themes.text: Gli Imprenditori italiani, interessati ad avviare attività economico-commerciali o ad effettuare investimenti, sono invitati a rivolgersi all’ Ambasciata d’Italia a Niamey.

themes.text: country-security"> Stand 19.07.2026 (Unverändert gültig seit: 07.05.2026) Sicherheitsstufe Sicherheitsstufe 4 seit 31.01.2018 Landkarte Reisewarnung (Sicherheitsstufe 4) im ganzen Land! Es wird vor allen Reisen nach Niger gewarnt. Die Sicherheitssituation für Ausländer ist zurzeit äußerst kritisch. Anschläge und Entführungen können, auch in der Hauptstadt Niamey, jederzeit vorkommen. Die Sicherheitslage kann sich jederzeit verändern und entwickeln. Daher empfehlen wir Ihnen: Nützen Sie unsere Auslandsservice-App sowie die kostenlose Reiseregistrierung! Informieren Sie sich über die Sicherheitslage vor Ort und überprüfen Sie diese regelmäßig! themes.heading: Sicherheit & Kriminalität
themes.text: Hinweise: Reisewarnungen werden im Regelfall nur in besonderen Krisensituationen ausgesprochen, wenn eine generelle Gefährdung für Leib und Leben besteht. Reisende, die sich in ein Gebiet mit Reisewarnung begeben, müssen sich der möglichen Konsequenzen bewusst sein: Konsularische Hilfeleistung für in Not geratene Österreicherinnen und Österreicher ist nur äußerst eingeschränkt möglich. Bei Reisen in Gebiete mit Reisewarnung können Versicherungen trotz eines aufrechten Versicherungsvertrages Ausschlussgründe geltend machen und sind damit leistungsfrei. Das Konsulargebührengesetz ermächtigt die Republik Österreich, die Kosten für allenfalls erforderliche Schutzmaßnahmen bzw. Hilfeleistung in bestimmten Situationen im Regressweg von Reisenden zurückzufordern! Notrufnummern: Ambulanz: 15 Feuerwehr: 18 Polizei: 17

themes.text: Still current at:19 July 2026 Updated:24 June 2026 Latest update:We continue to advise do not travel to Niger due to the volatile security environment, including the high risk of terrorism, kidnapping and violent crime. There's a high risk of kidnapping targeting foreigners. Terrorists may target places visited by foreigners, airports, government buildings and security facilities. There's ongoing political instability and conflict in Niger. Airports and borders may close suddenly. If you decide to remain, you're responsible for your own safety. Australia has no Embassy or Consulate in Niger. Our ability to provide any consular services is severely limited (see 'Safety'). We continue to advise: Do not travel to Niger due to the volatile security environment, including the high risk of terrorism, kidnapping and violent crime. Download Niger Download Africa

themes.heading: Safety
themes.text: There's ongoing political instability and conflict in Niger following a military takeover in 2023. If you're in Niger despite our advice, you should consider leaving. If you decide to remain, have plans in place to keep yourself safe and be ready to leave at short notice. Protests and civil unrest could occur and turn violent quickly without notice. Avoid government buildings, demonstrations, large crowds and political gatherings. The overall security situation remains highly volatile, and movement restrictions may be implemented at short notice. There's a high risk of kidnapping across Niger. Foreigners have been targeted. In 2025, a number of foreigners were kidnapped in Niger. You could be kidnapped from your residence, vehicle or workplace. There's a high risk of terrorism in Niger. Several terrorist groups operate in Niger. Terrorists could target foreigners and the places they visit, including hotels, cafes, restaurants, airports, government buildings, and security facilities. Stay alert and ensure you have security plans in place. Flight disruptions and airport closures can happen with little or no warning. Flights may be diverted to other ‘do not travel’ locations such as Burkina Faso or Mali. Some land borders remain closed, and others may close without warning. Trying to leave Niger by land borders is likely to be dangerous. Do not travel to several countries neighbouring Niger, including Burkina Faso, Mali, Libya, and Chad. We also advise do not travel to the border areas with Algeria, Nigeria and Benin. Violent crime, including armed home invasions, carjacking and mugging, is common. Don't travel alone. Travelling after dark brings increased risk. Full travel advice: Safety

themes.heading: Health
themes.text: Yellow fever and malaria are common. Ensure your accommodation is insect-proof. Use insect repellent. Get vaccinated against yellow fever before you travel. Consider taking anti-malarial medication. The HIV/AIDS infection rate is high. Take precautions if you engage in high-risk activities. Waterborne, foodborne and other infectious diseases are common. These include cholera, rabies, hepatitis, polio and bilharzia. Drink only boiled or bottled water. Avoid raw or undercooked food. Don't swim in fresh water. Ensure your polio vaccinations are up to date. Medical facilities in Niamey, including emergency help, may be limited and poor quality. Outside the capital, they're inadequate or non-existent. If you're seriously ill or injured, you'll need medical evacuation. Get full travel insurance that covers medical costs and evacuation. You'll probably need a special policy that covers travel to high-risk places. Most Australian policies won't cover you for travel to Niger. Full travel advice: Health

themes.heading: Local laws
themes.text: Don't use, carry or traffic illegal drugs. Severe penalties include heavy fines and long prison sentences, especially in areas where Islamic militants are implementing Sharia law. Always carry your passport with a valid visa or residence permit. Be careful when taking photos. Photographing military and government buildings, personnel, key infrastructure, and political demonstrations is illegal. Ask permission before taking pictures of locals. Same-sex relationships are illegal in Niger. Penalties include prison sentences of 5 to 10 years and fines. The laws also extend to people who officiate, witness, organise or consent to same-sex marriages. Niger doesn't recognise dual nationality. If you enter the country on your Nigerien passport, authorities will treat you as a Nigerien citizen. Always travel on your Australian passport. Dress and behaviour standards in Niger are conservative. Always dress and act appropriately. Full travel advice: Local laws

themes.heading: Travel
themes.text: If, despite our advice, you travel to Niger, you'll need a visa to enter the country. Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice. You should contact the nearest embassy or consulate of Niger for the latest details. You must show your yellow fever vaccination certificate to enter the country. Authorities have been conducting increased security checks on foreigners arriving in Niger by air. This may result in your passport being held by authorities for several days when you arrive in Niger. Some land ports of entry may be closed due to security considerations. Several roads in Niger are closed for travel, except with special authorisation. Without these authorisations, you could be arrested and deported. Landmines are a risk throughout the country. Drive only in daylight and drive in a convoy with adequate security. Taxis are old and in poor condition. Don't use public transport due to poor road conditions and the risk of violent crime. Hire a private car with a professional driver and consider engaging appropriate security support. If you travel outside Niamey, you must be escorted by Nigerien security or military forces. Full travel advice: Travel

themes.heading: Local contacts
themes.text: There are limits to what consular assistance we can provide in high-risk locations. Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas. Our ability to provide consular services in Niger is extremely limited. Australia doesn't have an embassy or consulate in Niger. You can access limited consular services from the Australian High Commission in Nigeria. Contact the High Commission to ask about its services and to make an appointment. Full travel advice: Local contacts

themes.heading: Political Instability
themes.text: There's ongoing political instability in Niger following a military takeover in July 2023. An interim military administration is in place. There's a heightened military presence around government buildings, including the Presidential Palace, and critical infrastructure. The security situation remains highly volatile. Some countries have ordered the departure of non-emergency government employees and their families. Australia has no Embassy or Consulate in Niger. Our ability to provide any consular or passport assistance is severely limited. Terrorists may target places visited by foreigners, government buildings and security facilities. Airports may be targeted and borders may close suddenly. Flight disruptions and airport closures can happen with little or no warning. Some land borders remain closed, and others may close without warning. Trying to leave Niger by land borders is likely to be dangerous. Do not travel to several countries neighbouring Niger, including Burkina Faso, Chad, Libya and Mali. We also advise do not travel to the border areas with Algeria, Benin and Nigeria. More information: Travel advice for Algeria Travel advice for Benin Travel advice for Burkina Faso Travel advice for Chad Travel advice for Libya Travel advice for Mali If you can't depart or decide to stay in Niger, limit your movements and avoid protests. Keep a supply of water, food and medicine. Stay in contact with family and friends.

themes.heading: Demonstrations and protests
themes.text: Public protests and events that draw large groups of people can turn violent without warning. Security forces have used force to break up protests. Demonstrations often occur near: government buildings universities public parks Mobile phone and internet connections may be disrupted during civil unrest. Movement restrictions may be implemented. Clashes between security forces and armed groups have happened in many regions of Niger. Several have initiated states of emergency in response. Curfews may be in place in some towns. They can change at short notice. If despite our advice you travel to Niger: avoid political rallies, protests, demonstrations and large gatherings if caught up in a protest, leave as soon as it's safe monitor international and reliable local media for possible unrest obey curfews follow the advice of local authorities More information: Demonstrations and civil unrest

themes.heading: Terrorism
themes.text: There's a threat of terrorism in Niger, including in the capital, Niamey. Attacks could be indiscriminate and occur without warning in places visited by foreigners. Several terrorist groups operate in Niger. Many attacks have occurred in recent years, and more attacks are likely. Be aware of your surroundings, be alert to threats and exercise caution, especially around: hospitality venues, including hotels, cafes, restaurants and bars locations popular with tourists and foreigners, including national parks transport hubs, including airports religious sites and places of worship sporting and cultural events shopping centres, supermarkets and markets government buildings security facilities international organisations diplomatic buildings police stations If there's a terrorist attack: follow the advice of local authorities leave the area as soon as it's safe avoid large crowds and the affected area in case of secondary attacks If you decide to travel to Niger despite our advice: get professional security advice arrange suitable security measures monitor international and reliable local media and other sources for threats avoid possible targets have an exit plan if you need to visit an area that's a possible target stay in accommodation with appropriate security measures in place report suspicious activity or items to police take official warnings seriously More information: Terrorism

themes.heading: Kidnapping
themes.text: Kidnapping occurs across the world with political, ideological and criminal motives. Foreigners, including Australians, have been kidnapped overseas whilst travelling. Kidnaps can happen anywhere, even in places that seem safe. There’s a very high risk of kidnapping in Niger, including in the capital Niamey. Various terrorist and criminal groups are active in Niger and neighbouring countries. Several foreigners have been kidnapped in Niger, including in 2025. You could be kidnapped from your residence, vehicle or workplace. Foreign nationals and foreign interests, including mining operations, may be targeted. Tourists, aid workers, journalists and long-term residents may be seen as lucrative targets. Kidnappers often conduct extensive planning prior to a kidnapping and may leverage local networks. Kidnappers may execute hostages. If you travel to Niger despite our advice, our ability to provide consular assistance is extremely limited. You’re responsible for your own safety. You can find more information on how to reduce your risk of kidnapping here: Kidnapping. The Australian Government's longstanding policy is that it doesn't make payments or concessions to kidnappers. Ransom payments to kidnappers have funded further terrorist attacks and criminal activity. Ransom payments to terrorist groups are likely to contravene Australian counter-terrorism financing laws. More information: Scams that affect travellers

themes.heading: Violent crime
themes.text: Violent crime is common across Niger, including in Niamey. Crimes include: armed home invasions kidnapping carjackings muggings The risk of violent crime increases at night, but it can happen at any time. We advise against walking alone, even in the vicinity of your hotel. Criminals often work in groups to target foreigners and 4WD vehicles. Outside central Niamey, the threat of violent crime is higher.

themes.heading: Petty crime
themes.text: Pickpocketing and other petty crimes are common in Niger, including Niamey. If, despite our advice, you travel to Niger: pay close attention to your personal belongings avoid walking in isolated areas or along dark streets at night lock your accommodation, even when you're inside keep car windows and doors locked, even when moving limit any displays of personal wealth.

themes.heading: Scams and fraud
themes.text: Internet scams come in many forms, including romance, friendship, business and job offers. Once the online friendship develops, your friend or prospective marriage partner might ask you to send money so they can travel to Australia. Criminals might also: ask to transfer large amounts of money to an overseas bank account and offer you a percentage provide fake cashier cheques for urgent shipments of goods ask for large fees for fake government contracts lure you to Niger for a business opportunity You could be lured to Africa to meet your friend. Once in Africa, you could become the victim of kidnapping, assault, robbery, or extortion. Some victims have been killed. Don't travel to Niger to get your money back or to get revenge. If you suspect a scam, get legal advice. Some scams ask you to transfer money through the Australian High Commission in Abuja, Nigeria. Some scammers pretend to work there. Check with the High Commission if you're asked to send money or details to them (see 'Local contacts'). To protect yourself from scams: be wary of online contact from people you don't know don't send money to anyone until you've thoroughly checked them If, despite our advice, you travel to Niger, warn your friends and family about scams relating to your welfare. They may get fake phone calls and emails claiming you're in legal, financial or medical trouble and asking for money to help. While you're in Niger, be cautious about giving out personal information. Don't throw away personal documents.

themes.heading: Extreme weather and natural disasters
themes.text: The rainy season is from June to October. Flooding and landslides may occur, blocking roads. The dry season is from November to May. During the dry season, Niger may be affected by the 'Harmattan' seasonal wind. This seasonal wind blows a lot of sand and dust in the air. Visibility may be very limited. This wind can cause problems for people with respiratory or optical issues. If extreme weather or a natural disaster occurs: monitor reliable local media follow the advice of local authorities keep in touch with friends and family Register with the Global Disaster Alert and Coordination System to receive alerts on major disasters.

themes.heading: Travel insurance
themes.text: You need special travel insurance if you’re going to a high-risk destination, which can be expensive. We can’t guarantee your safety and security in another country or pay for your medical expenses, including evacuation. If you don’t have insurance, you shouldn’t travel.

themes.heading: Physical and mental health
themes.text: Travelling overseas can be stressful and uncertain. You may experience unfamiliar environments, travel disruptions, changes in normal routines and limited access to health care. Not taking your prescribed medications is a common cause of mental ill-health for Australians overseas. If you are concerned about your welfare, or someone else's, contact your nearest Australian embassy, high commission, or consulate. You can also call the 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre on +61 2 6261 3305. Mental health resources: Information and advice on mental illness (HealthDirect). Living with mental illness (BeyondBlue) Medicare Mental Health (the Department of Health and Aged Care) More information: General health advice Mental health support overseas

themes.heading: Insect-borne diseases
themes.text: Yellow fever is widespread in Niger. Yellow fever is a potentially fatal virus spread by mosquitoes. It's preventable by vaccination. Get vaccinated for before you travel. Insect-borne diseases, including malaria and dengue, are common. To protect yourself from disease: make sure your accommodation is insect-proof use insect repellent wear long, loose, light-coloured clothing consider taking medicine to prevent malaria Get medical advice if you have a fever, muscle pain, rash or severe headache.

themes.heading: HIV/AIDS
themes.text: The HIV/AIDS infection rate is high in Niger. Take precautions if you engage in activities that may expose you to the virus.

themes.heading: Other health risks
themes.text: Waterborne, foodborne and other infectious diseases are common, including: cholera diphtheria hepatitis polio (poliomyelitis) rabies tuberculosis typhoid bilharzia (schistosomiasis) Serious outbreaks can occur. To protect yourself from illness: drink boiled water or bottled water with sealed lids avoid ice cubes avoid raw and undercooked food, such as salads don't swim in fresh water Get medical advice if you have a fever or diarrhoea. Ask your doctor about health risks and vaccines before you go. More information: Infectious diseases

themes.heading: Medical care
themes.text: There’s no agreement between Australia and Niger for free healthcare. Tourists must pay for all medical care, even if they need to stay in hospital or be evacuated. This can be very expensive. Make sure your travel insurance covers you. The Australian Government won't pay for these costs. Medical facilities in Niamey, including emergency help, are limited and of poor quality. Outside the capital, facilities are inadequate, non-existent or under the control of armed militias. You'll probably need to pay up-front for medical services. If you can't, this may delay your treatment. You'll need to be evacuated if you get seriously ill or injured. Medical evacuation may not be possible and can be very expensive. Most Australian travel insurance policies won't cover you for travel to Niger.

themes.heading: Drugs
themes.text: Penalties for possessing, using or trafficking illegal drugs can include heavy fines and/or long jail sentences in Niger. More information: Carrying or using drugs

themes.heading: Other laws
themes.text: The death penalty applies to serious crimes, such as murder. Always carry your passport with a valid visa or residence permit. NGOs must register with the Government of Niger before they start their mission. If you work without permission, you could be detained and deported. It's illegal to take photos of: military personnel, zones and assets radio and television stations airports the Presidency Building diplomatic buildings the Kennedy Bridge in Niamey political demonstrations

themes.heading: LGBTQIA+ information
themes.text: Same-sex relationships are illegal in Niger. Penalties include prison sentences of 5 to 10 years and fines. This applies to individuals who are found guilty of what is described as ‘LGBTQIA+ acts’. The laws also extend to people who officiate, witness, organise or consent to same-sex marriages. LGBTQIA+ travellers should consider travel plans and personal security carefully. More information: Advice for LGBTQIA+ travellers

themes.heading: Local customs
themes.text: Respect religious and cultural customs and laws. Ask for permission before taking photos of locals.

themes.heading: Dress standards
themes.text: Standards of dress and behaviour are generally conservative. Take care not to offend. If you're a woman, consider wearing a headscarf and loose clothes that cover your arms and legs, especially where locals are doing so. If you're not sure, get local advice.

themes.heading: Ramadan
themes.text: The Islamic holy month of Ramadan is observed in Niger. It's illegal to eat, drink and smoke in public during daylight hours in Ramadan. If you're not fasting, avoid eating, drinking or smoking in front of people who are. Explore our Ramadan page to learn more, including dates for Ramadan.

themes.heading: Visas and border measures
themes.text: Each country or territory decides its own entry and exit rules, including health border screening measures. To find out what you need to enter a destination, ask their embassy, consulate or immigration office.

themes.heading: Visas
themes.text: If, despite our advice, you travel to Niger, you'll need a visa in advance of travel to enter the country.

themes.heading: Border measures
themes.text: Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice. Contact the nearest embassy or consulate of Niger for details about visas, currency, customs and quarantine rules. Authorities have been conducting increased security checks on foreigners arriving in Niger by air. This may result in your passport being held by authorities for several days when you arrive in Niger.

themes.heading: Yellow fever vaccination
themes.text: You'll need a valid yellow fever vaccination certificate to enter Niger. Some airlines may ask to see it before boarding a flight to or from Niger. Find out about returning to Australia after exposure to yellow fever. More information: Countries with a risk of yellow fever

themes.heading: Lost or stolen passport
themes.text: Your passport is valuable and can be used by others to commit crimes. Don’t let anyone trick you into giving it to them. Always keep it in a safe place. If your passport is lost or stolen, tell the Australian Government: In Australia: contact the Australian Passport Information Service. Overseas: contact the nearest Australian embassy or consulate.

themes.heading: Passport with 'X' gender identifier
themes.text: Australian passports comply with international standards for sex and gender. If your passport has an 'X' in the sex field, we can’t guarantee your entry or transit to other countries. Ask the embassy or consulate of your destinations if they’ll accept your passport with an 'X' gender marker. Check all transit destinations too. More information: LGBTQIA+ travellers

themes.heading: Money
themes.text: The local currency is the West African CFA Franc (XOF). Only change money at a reputable exchange bureau. Australian dollars aren't accepted. You can convert US dollars and euros to XOF. Cash traveller's cheques at commercial banks. You'll need to show the original receipt and your passport. Be aware that: Niger is a cash-based society ATMs are not readily available credit cards are rarely accepted, even in major hotels and restaurants Bring enough cash to cover your needs.

themes.heading: Landmines
themes.text: Unexploded weapons, improvised explosive devices and landmines are a risk throughout the country. Armed groups are known to target convoys travelling on major routes using such weapons.

themes.heading: Driving permit
themes.text: You can drive in Niger with a valid Australian driver's licence (digital driver's licences may not be accepted) and an International Driving Permit (IDP). You must get your IDP before you leave Australia. You must get a local licence if you're staying longer than 3 months.

themes.heading: Road travel
themes.text: Other than a few main routes, most roads are in bad condition or unsealed, including within Niamey. Dangers include: poor vehicle maintenance poor street lighting hazardous local driving habits Roads can be dangerous, with traffic including bicycles pedestrians animals tractors Checkpoints are common across Niger, including in Niamey. If you travel outside Niamey, you must be escorted by Nigerien security or military forces. Carry your vehicle registration and ownership papers, as well as necessary permits. Authorities may ask for these at any time, especially outside main towns. There's no roadside assistance. Before you drive, check your insurance covers you and learn local traffic laws and practices. If despite our advice you have to travel by road: seek professional security advice and arrange appropriate security measures drive in a convoy and with adequate security, including the use of armoured vehicles carry sufficient fuel and drinking water for longer than your intended journey avoid driving at night carry photo ID, vehicle registration and ownership papers keep car windows up and doors locked, even when moving stay on marked roads carry a satellite phone and first-aid kit More information: Driving or riding

themes.heading: Motorcycles
themes.text: Ensure your travel insurance covers you when using a motorbike, quad bike or similar vehicle. Always wear a helmet.

themes.heading: Taxis
themes.text: Don't use local taxis. They are generally old and in poor condition. Ride-sharing services are unreliable. Both have poor safety standards and pose a risk of petty crime. We recommend hiring a private car with a professional driver. Travelling in an armoured vehicle is the safest option.

themes.heading: Public transport
themes.text: Don't use public transport. It is often unsafe due to unqualified and uninsured drivers, poor vehicle maintenance and overcrowding. Roads are in poor condition, and there's a risk of violent crime or terrorism. Public transport has been targeted by criminal gangs and armed groups in the past.

themes.heading: Air travel
themes.text: DFAT doesn’t give advice about how safe different airlines or flight paths are. Safety risks and weather events may cause flight delays and cancellations. Contact your travel agent or airline to confirm your plans. Airlines and airports can restrict what you can take in your luggage. Regulations may be different to those in Australia. Check with your airline before you travel. Check Niger's air safety profile with the Aviation Safety Network. Dust storms are common during the 'Harmattan' season (December to February). Flights can be re-routed when visibility is poor. More information: Air travel

themes.heading: Medical emergencies
themes.text: Call 15 or 20 72 31 41 and ask for an ambulance or go directly to a hospital. Ambulances may be slow to respond.

themes.heading: Consular contacts
themes.text: There are limits to what consular assistance we can provide in high-risk locations. Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas. Australia doesn't have an embassy or consulate in Niger. You can access limited consular services from the Australian High Commission in Nigeria. The Australian High Commission in Abuja has very limited capacity to provide consular assistance in Niger.

themes.heading: 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre
themes.text: In a consular emergency, if you can't contact an embassy, call the 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre on: +61 2 6261 3305 from overseas 1300 555 135 in Australia

themes.heading: 사건ㆍ사고 현황
themes.text: [정정 상황 (전쟁·내란·테러 등)] ㅇ 폭력적 극단주의 단체에 의한 테러 및 납치 발생 가능성 상존 - 니제르의 프랑스 주도 말리 평화유지활동 참여, 사헬지역 보코하람 격퇴 작전 개입 등에 따라 테러리스트 단체의 공격 표적이 되고 있는 상황 ㅇ 니제르 수도인 니아메 지역 내 시위 증가추세 [범죄] ㅇ 극도로 열악한 빈곤 사정으로 인해 수도 니아메에서도 생계형 범죄 발생 가능성 상존 - 도난, 강도 및 주거 침입은 언제든지 발생할 수 있으며, 외국인 관광객들이 주요 표적 - 외국인 대상으로 절도 사건이 주로 발생 가능하므로, 돈, 여권, 보석, 휴대전화와 같은 중요한 물품들은 항상 안전하게 보관하는 것이 필요 - 니아메 내 호텔, 박물관 등 관광객이 많이 찾는 지역은 범죄 발생 가능성이 높으므로 특히 주의 필요 - 으슥한 길을 혼자 걷는 것은 삼가는 것이 좋고, 가로등이 부족하므로 야간에 단독 보행 또한 위험 ㅇ 차량 대상 범죄도 종종 발생하므로 차 문이 항상 잠겨있는지 확인 ㅇ 국경 지역에서는 특히 야간에 강도, 밀수 및 기타 범죄행위가 흔하게 발생 - 강도단들은 니제르 남부 진더와 나이지리아와의 국경 지역에서 활동하는 것으로 파악되고 있으므로 동 지역을 통해 국경을 넘나드는 일은 삼갈것 [사기] ㅇ 거액의 자금을 해외로 반출하는 데 도움을 주면 일정 비율을 분배해주겠다는 제의나, 금, 다이아몬드를 값싼 가격으로 살 수 있다는 등의 제의와 같이 상식선에서 보았을 때 과도한 이익을 보장하는 제안이나 거래는 일단 의심을 가지고 신중하게 판단 필요 [테러] ㅇ 니제르는 말리, 나이지리아 등에 근거지를 둔 테러 단체들의 위협에 노출되어 있고, 리비아 접경지역에서는 무기밀수, 인신매매가 이루어지고 있는 등 접경지대에서 이슬람 테러단체의 위협이 확대되고 있는 상황 - 2021.3.17 니제르-말리 국경 인근 Tillaberi 지역 신원미상의 무장괴환이 출현, 무차별 공격을 감행하여 민간이 58명 사망자등 발생 - 2021.2.21. 니제르-말리 국경 인근 Tillaberi 지역에서 대선 2차 투표 감독을 위해 이동하던 차량이 지뢰로 폭파, 선거관리위원회(CENI) 소속 직원 10명 사상자 발생 - 2021.1월 대선 1차 투표직후 Tillaberi 지역 내 두 마을에서 100명 이상의 시민이 이슬람 극단주의 세력으로부터 살해되는 사건 발생 - 2019.12.10. 말리 접경 Inate 지역에서 자살폭탄, 트럭 및 중화기 사용 등 테러 공격으로 니제르군 71명 사망 등 ㅇ 테러리스트에 의한 무차별 공격이 발생할 가능성이 높으므로, 외국인, 특히 서양인이 자주 찾는 호텔, 레스토랑, 교회 등의 장소에서는 신변안전에 각별히 주의 필요 - 2020.8.18. 수도 니아메(Niamey)에서 남쪽으로 60km 떨어진 쿠아레(kouaré) 자연생태공원에서 프랑스인 6명 및 현지인 2명 총 8명이 외국인을 겨냥한 테러로 추정되는 공격으로 사망 [납치] ㅇ 이슬람국가서부아프리카지부(ISWA, Islamic State West Africa), 보코하람 등 폭력적 극단주의 단체에 의한 납치 위협 다대 - 동 단체들은 말리, 부르키나파소, 알제리, 리비아 등 니제르와 인접한 모든 국가의 접경 지역에서 활동중 - 외국인, 특히 서양인들의 납치 피해 사례가 많은바, 2018년 9월 카톨릭 성직자가 수도 니아메에서 남서쪽으로 125km 떨어진 Makolondi 지역에서 납치되었으며, 2018년 4월에는 개발원조 관계자가 Tillaberi 지역에서 납치 ㅇ 이처럼 니제르 지역에서 일을 하거나 여행을 하는 경우 테러리스트에 의한 납치 위험이 상존하므로, 교회, 시장, 쇼핑몰, 레스토랑, 호텔 바와 같은 사람이 많이 모이는 지역, 특히 혼잡한 장소에서는 매우 각별한 유의 필요 ㅇ 지난 10년간 니제르를 포함한 사헬지역에서 활동하는 여러 테러 단체에 의해 관광객, NGO 활동가, 외교관 등 많은 외국인이 납치된 바 있으며, 일부는 아직까지도 석방되지 못하고 있는 상태이고, 일부는 피살 - 개발원조사업, 언론 등에 종사하는 외국인들이 주요 표적이 되고 있으며, 높은 몸값을 통한 금전적 이익 추구가 주요 납치 목적 - 2020.10.27. 미국인 선교사 납치, 10.31 귀환 - 2018년 9월 카톨릭 신부 수도 니아메에서 남서쪽으로 125km 떨어진 Bamongo에서 납치, 2020.10월 귀환 - 2018년 4월 개발원조 활동가 Tillaberi 지역에서 납치, 현재까지 미귀환 - 2016년 미국인 선교사 겸 구호사업 활동가 납치, 현재까지 미귀환 ㅇ 교회, 시장, 쇼핑몰, 레스토랑, 호텔 바와 같은 사람이 많이 모이는 지역, 특히 혼잡한 장소에서는 매우 각별한 유의 필요

themes.heading: 자연재해
themes.text: ㅇ 니제르는 사막기후이며 가뭄이 심하므로, 많은 물을 섭취하여야 탈수를 방지할 수 있습니다. ㅇ 11월~2월 사이 건기에는 아르마탕(Harmattan)이라고 하는 먼지바람(황사)이 사하라 사막으로부터 불어와서, 호흡기 장애를 일으킬 수 있고 특히 뇌수막염의 원인이 되기도 하므로, 이 시기에 여행하는 경우 뇌수막염 예방접종이 바람직함 ㅇ 물에는 거머리가 많으므로 소독된 수영장 이외의 곳에서는 수영을 삼가야 합니다.

themes.heading: 유의해야 할 지역
themes.text: ㅇ 수도 니아메이에서는 가웨이(Gaweye) 호텔, 국립박물관, 케네디 다리, 쁘띠 마르쉐 주변에서 소매치기와 날치기를 주의하여야 합니다. ㅇ Abalak부터 북부 지역은 투아레그 활동 지역으로서 Air Massif 지역, Iferouane, Arlit, Agadez 등의 북부 도시는 방문하지 않는 것이 좋습니다. ㅇ 사하라 사막을 넘어 북부 리비아, 알제리를 육로로 통행하는 것은 투아레그나 무장강도의 위험뿐만 아니라 길을 잃을 수도 있기 때문에 바람직하지 않습니다. - 사막 지역을 반드시 통행해야 할 이유가 있을 때는 안내자와 함께 GPS와 위성통신 장비를 갖추고 다녀야 합니다. ㅇ 도시 이외의 지역은 치안상태가 좋지 않으므로 안내자를 대동하고 여행하는 것이 좋습니다.

themes.heading: 출입국 (in- en uitreis)
themes.text: 무비자 입국 가능 여부(1회 입국시 최대 연속 체류 가능 일자) ㅇ 외교관 여권 소지자 : X ㅇ 관용여권 소지자 : X ㅇ 일반여권 소지자(복수와 단수를 구분할 경우 별도 표기 필요) : X ㅇ 도착사증 발급 여부 : X [통과여객 조건] - 공항 내에서 24시간 혹은 당일 단순 경유시 별도 조건은 없으나, 경유시 니제르에 입국하는 경우 경유사증 필요 입국시 유의사항 등 ㅇ 니제르 정부가 새로 발표('25.1.13.)한 외국인 입국 및 체류에 관한 규정에 근거해 입국시 유효한 사증(비자) 및 체류증을 소지 하지 않을 경우 추방, 송환, 행정 구금, 국경 강제 이송 등 행정조치 시행 출국시 유의사항 등 ㅇ 문화재 반출시 반출허가증을 사전에 받아야 함 참고자료 ㅇ 해당 국가의 주한공관홈페이지 : 주한니제르명예영사관 http://nigerkorea.org ㅇ 해당국가의 한국 대사관 홈페이지 : https://overseas.mofa.go.kr/ci-ko/index.do - 니제르 정부가 입국시 신규 사증 발급 요건 및 제출 서류 목록을 발표한바('25.1.21.), 사증 신청시 주한니제르명예영사관 및 거주지 소재 니제르 대사관에 확인 필요 ㅇ 기타 참고자료 : - 해당국 외교부 : www.diplomatie.gouv.ne

themes.heading: 일반문화
themes.text: ㅇ 공용어는 불어이나 도시 이외 지역에서는 부족어를 사용하기 때문에 불어가 안 통하는 경우 다대 ㅇ 관공서, 레스토랑, 상점 등에서의 일처리가 한국에 비해 매우 느리므로, 인내심 필요 ㅇ 대부분의 사람들이 사진 촬영을 좋아하나, 일부 부정적으로 여기는 사람들이 있으므로 사전에 허락을 구하는 등 예의를 지키는 것이 좋음 ㅇ 니제르에서는 손님에 대한 접대를 매우 중요하게 여기기 때문에, 상대방의 친절을 거절하지 않고 공손하게 받는 것이 좋으며, 음식 등을 거절해야만 하는 경우에는 상당히 신중하게 의사표시 필요 [현지 법과 관습] ㅇ 니제르는 무슬림 국가이므로 지역 전통, 관습, 법률 및 종교를 항상 존중하는 자세 필요 ㅇ 왼손은 화장실에서 사용하는 더러운 손으로 인식되어 있기 때문에, 왼손으로 악수하는 거나 식사를 해서는 안되며, 노출이 심한 옷차림을 하였을 때는 매우 부정적으로 보이므로 주의 필요 ㅇ 군대 또는 정부 시설을 사진 촬영하는 것은 삼갈 것 ㅇ 항상 신분증(여권 등)을 지참해야 하며, 형사 범죄를 저지르는 경우에는 현지 법에 따라 처벌을 받게 되며, 지역 교도소 상황은 매우 열악 ㅇ 니제르의 군경은 정당한 이유 없이 48시간 이내 구금이 가능하므로, 검문시 협조 필요

themes.heading: 주요 교통 법규 및 문화
themes.text: ㅇ 국제운전면허 인정(국제면허증에 기재된 유효기간 만큼 인정) ㅇ 운전석 위치 : 한국과 동일 ㅇ 운전시 핸드폰 사용이 금지되며, 적발시 범칙금 부과 ㅇ 장의 차량(일반 트럭이나 픽업 트럭에 시신을 싣고 전조등이나 비상등을 켜고 그 뒤에 긴 행렬의 차량이 뒤따름)행렬이 지나가는 경우 반대 차선의 차량은 운행을 멈추는 것이 교통문화 ㅇ 낮에는 응급 상황을 제외하고는 경찰과 군대 차량만 조명등(헤드 라이트) 점등 가능

themes.heading: 관련 사건사고 사례
themes.text: ㅇ 경찰이 현장 조사 내역을 근거로 작성한 사고 경위서(P verbal)를 보험회사에 제출하여 보험금을 지급받는 데 상기 과정이 매우 길고 인내심을 요합니다.

themes.heading: 관련 사건사고 발생시 대처방법
themes.text: [대중교통] ㅇ 수도에서 교통수단은 택시이며, 요금은 일반적으로 합승 시에 200세파(한화 4백원), 그 이상은 미리 가격 협상 - 보통 장거리의 경우에는 400세파(한화 8백원), 공항에서 시내까지는 3,000~5,000CFA(야간; 10,000 CFA) ㅇ 호텔 앞에는 일반 차량을 이용하여 외국인을 대상으로 택시 영업을 하는 차량들이 있으며 가격이 매우 비쌈 - 시내 이용 시 시간당 5,000세파(한화 1만원)이며 하루 종일 이용할 경우 약 50,000세파(시외 및 지방 이동 요금은 별도 합의 필요) [도로 및 교통 여건] ㅇ 도로를 포함한 모든 인프라의 유지, 보수가 잘 되지 않고 있으며 운전 여건이 좋지 못하므로, 여행자는 자가운전을 삼가는 것이 좋음 ㅇ 주요 사고 원인으로는 운전자들의 부주의, 과속, 차량 결함 등이며, 도로와 조명 사정이 좋지 않아 야간 운전은 특히 위험 - 도로 곳곳에 트럭, 버스 등이 고장난 채로 서 있는 경우가 많으며, 수도 니아메의 경우 교통 신호등이 제대로 작동하지 않거나, 교통 표지판이 누락되거나 손상되어 있는 경우도 종종 발생 ㅇ 사고가 발생한 경우 현장에 남아 있으면 지역 주민에 의한 피해가 발생할 가능성이 있으므로, 사고 발생 즉시 가까운 경찰서를 방문하여 신고하는 것이 좋음

themes.heading: 기타 유의사항
themes.text: ㅇ 시내 중심가를 제외하면 대부분 야간 조명용 가로등이 없으므로 야간 운전을 삼가하는 것이 바람직합니다.

themes.heading: 날씨
themes.text: ㅇ 북부지방은 사막기후로서 매우 덥고 강수량이 연중 20mm 미만입니다. 남쪽으로 내려올수록 강우량이 많아지고 수도 니아메이 부근은 900mm 정도입니다. ㅇ 우기는 7~8월로서, 기온은 23도~33도이며, 나머지 기간은 비가 잘 오지 않는 건기로서 낮에 매우 덥고 일교차가 심합니다. (15도~43도)

themes.heading: 기타
themes.text: [주요 질병] ㅇ HIV/AIDS, 말라리아, 장티푸스 등 ㅇ 2020.3월 코로나19 첫 확진자 발생 후 2021.4.15기준 현재 5,081명 누적 확진자, 189명 누적 사망자 발생 * 코로나19 백신접종은 2021.4.15. 현재 국내 미승인 백신 시노팜 백신 접종중. 조만간 국제사회 지원에 따른 여타 백신 수급 예정 ㅇ 성인 인구의 약 0.8%가 에이즈 감염자로 파악되고 있는 바, 에이즈에 노출되지 않도록 주의 필요 ㅇ 여행 전 국내 의료 기관을 통해 국가별 최신 질병 정보 확인 필요 ㅇ 질병관리청보건부 홈페이지 등을 통해 여행전 필요한 예방접종 등 질병예방 정보 확인 [의료여건] ㅇ 말라리아, 장염, 감기 등 기초적인 질병은 현지에 소재한 병원에서 진료 및 치료가 가능하나 수술이 필요한 중병은 프랑스나 한국으로의 전지 치료를 권장 ㅇ 만약 여행자보험을 들어오는 경우라면 말라리아 관련 치료도 보험에 포함되는지 확인 필요 ㅇ 수시로 손을 씻고, 음식물 섭취에 유의하는 등 위생 상태 항상 각별 유의 필요 [결제수단] ㅇ 니제르는 현금 기반 사회로, 최고의 호텔 및 레스토랑에서도 신용카드가 거의 통용되지 않으며, ATM 기계도 거의 없는 상황 [비자 및 출입국 관련] ㅇ 사전 확인 - 비자 및 출입국 요건 등 관련 정책이 조금씩 변동하는 경우가 있으므로 여행전 주한니제르 명예영사관 등에 문의 및 확인 필요 ㅇ 입국요건 - 사증(비자) 필요, 황열병 예방 접종 카드 필수 - 출입국 시 72시간 이내 발급받은 코로나19 PCR 음성확인서 제출 및 유증상 시 신속항체검사 및 PCR 검사 실시 - 14일 이상 체류하는 경우 7일간 자가격리 실시 * △연락 가능한 장소에서 자가격리 실시 필요, △격리 시작 시 경찰당국이 입국자의 여권을 회수하고 격리 종료 시 반환, △격리 7일 째에 PCR 검사를 실시하여 음성 판정시 격리 종료 * 단, 출장 등 목적의 13일 이하 단기 체류자의 경우 자가격리 불요하나 방역조치 준수 및 출국 항공권 소지 필요 - 여권 잔여 유효 기간 : 6개월 이상 - 단수여권 : 인정 / 여행증명서 : 불인정 - 통과여객시 : 무사증시 통과여객 가능하나, 공항 밖으로 외출 불가 ㅇ 주한니제르 명예영사관 - 한국에는 주한니제르대사관이 소재하지 않으며, 주중니제르대사관이 한국 겸임중인바, 주한니제르 명예영사관을 통해 비자 및 출입국 관련 문의 가능 - 주소 : 서울특별시 강남구 테헤란로 322, 동관 1110호 - 전화번호 : 02-2183-1109 - E-mail : nigerkorea@gmail.com ㅇ 비자 - 중국 주재 니제르 공관에서 사증 발급 신청 혹은 주한니제르 명예영사관 통해 대리 신청 - 비가 신청 구비서류 등 기타 자세한 사항은 주한니제르 명예영사관 홈페이지 등 통해 확인 가능
